blocks.md:
Let's design a **news aggregator** — a system that continuously pulls articles from thousands of external sources (RSS feeds, public APIs, scrapers), cleans and de-duplicates them, auto-categorizes/tags them, and serves them to millions of readers through `/latest`, `/trending`, `/category/:name`, and `/search`. The core tension: this is a **write-heavy ingestion pipeline bolted onto a brutally read-heavy serving tier** (~1,400 reads for every article we ingest), so the two halves want completely different architectures.

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, then draw the high-level design one requirement at a time — ingestion, categorization, the read feeds, and search — and finally go deep on the two or three hardest parts: deduplication, trending at scale, and keeping the read path fast. I'll check in with you as I go. Sound good?"

> 🏢 I'll target an **Amazon SDE-3 bar** — so I'll make **cost and operations** first-class (ingestion retries, on-call alarms, dedup correctness) and work backwards from the reader. Tell me if you'd rather I tilt toward raw scale (Meta) or one deep distributed-systems problem (Google) and I'll re-tilt.

---

## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

The core tension in one line: **we ingest hundreds of messy, duplicated articles a minute and must serve a clean, ranked, near-real-time feed to ~20M daily readers — the ingestion side is a correctness problem, the serving side is a latency problem, and they barely touch.**

### Functional (the core flows — "above the line")

**F1 — Ingest & normalize articles from many sources (with dedup).**
We poll RSS feeds, call public news APIs, and scrape where needed; we normalize every item into one schema (title, body, author, publishedAt, source, url) and drop duplicates/near-duplicates.
> 🗣️ Plain words: "We're constantly vacuuming up news from thousands of places that all format things differently, turning it into one clean shape, and throwing away the same story when ten sites all run it."
> ⚡ Why it's hard: the same wire story (e.g. a Reuters report) appears verbatim or lightly reworded across hundreds of outlets — detecting *near*-duplicates, not just exact ones, at hundreds/min is the real difficulty.

**F2 — Categorize & tag articles automatically.**
Classify each article into one or more categories (Politics, Sports, Tech) and attach tags (India, Elections, AI); an article can belong to many of each.
> 🗣️ Plain words: "Every story gets automatically filed under topics and labeled with keywords, so a reader can ask for just Sports, or just everything tagged AI."
> ⚡ Why it's hard: classification must run in-line at ingestion speed and be revisable — models improve, so we need to re-tag old content without blocking the live pipeline.

**F3 — Serve reader feeds: `/latest`, `/trending`, `/category/:name`, sorted by recency / popularity / relevance.**
Readers open the app and get fast, ranked lists; trending reflects what's hot *right now*.
> 🗣️ Plain words: "When you open the app you instantly see the newest stories, the ones everyone's reading right now, or a specific section — and it's snappy even though millions of people are doing the same thing."
> ⚡ Why it's hard: ~35K reads/sec at peak against constantly-changing data — we can't hit the database for every request, but `/trending` is inherently dynamic so it can't just be cached forever.

**F4 — Full-text search: `/search?q=...` sorted by relevance.**
Readers type a query and get ranked matching articles in well under a second.
> 🗣️ Plain words: "You can type 'red carpet oscars' and get the most relevant recent stories back almost instantly, ranked by how well they match."
> ⚡ Why it's hard: ranked full-text search across 100M+ articles in <200ms is something no relational `LIKE` query can do — it needs a dedicated inverted index.

**Below the line (out of scope for the main pass — happy to add):** user personalization / follow (called out as the F2 follow-up), comments, push notifications, paywalls/auth for premium, recommendation ML, mobile-offline sync. I'll treat **personalization** as a fast-follow because it reuses the same feed plumbing.

### Non-Functional

**N1 — Read availability: four nines (99.99%) on the feed/serving path.**
> 🗣️ Plain words: "The part readers touch is essentially always up — under ~1 hour of downtime a year."
> 💥 What breaks without it: a news app that's down during a breaking-news spike (election night, a disaster) is worthless precisely when traffic — and the value — peaks.

**N2 — Read latency: p99 < 200ms for `/latest` and `/category`; < 400ms for `/search`.**
> 🗣️ Plain words: "99 out of 100 feed loads come back in under a fifth of a second; search in under half a second."
> 💥 What breaks without it: slow feeds kill engagement instantly — readers bounce to a competitor, and ad/impression revenue drops with them.

**N3 — Ingestion freshness: a breaking story is visible to readers within ~1–2 minutes of publication.**
> 🗣️ Plain words: "When something big breaks, it shows up in the app a minute or two later, not an hour later."
> 💥 What breaks without it: a "news" product that's 30 minutes behind Twitter isn't news — freshness is the entire product promise.

**N4 — Ingestion reliability: no source silently stops; failed pulls are retried and alarmed.**
> 🗣️ Plain words: "If a source's feed goes down or starts erroring, we automatically retry and page someone — we never just quietly stop covering a publisher."
> 💥 What breaks without it: a feed breaks Friday night, nobody notices, and we're missing an entire publisher's coverage all weekend — invisible data loss.

**N5 — Dedup correctness: near-zero duplicate stories in a feed; near-zero false merges of distinct stories.**
> 🗣️ Plain words: "Readers shouldn't see the same story ten times, but we also must not accidentally merge two genuinely different stories into one."
> 💥 What breaks without it: a feed full of the same wire story looks broken and spammy; over-merging hides real coverage. Both erode trust.

**N6 — Cost-efficiency (Amazon lens): serve cheaply at read scale.**
> 🗣️ Plain words: "At a billion reads a day, the bill is dominated by serving bytes and search — we keep that lean."
> 💥 What breaks without it: an unbounded CDN/egress and Elasticsearch bill makes the unit economics underwater at scale.

> ⚠️ Gaps I'd flag out loud: scraping third-party sites raises legal/ToS and politeness (rate-limit) concerns I'd confirm with the interviewer; and "trending" needs an agreed definition (views? velocity? per-region?) before I size it. I'll revisit both in the ledger.

> 💬 "So the headline tension is two systems in a trenchcoat: a reliability-and-correctness ingestion pipeline, and a latency-and-cost serving tier. I'll keep them decoupled through an event log so each can scale on its own terms."

> 🎙️ Script: "At its core this is a read-heavy system — about fourteen hundred reads for every article we take in — so most of my effort goes into a cheap, fast read path. But the *interesting* problems live on the write side: de-duplicating the same story across hundreds of outlets, and auto-categorizing fast enough to stay within a minute or two of breaking news. My non-functionals are four-nines availability and sub-200-millisecond feeds on the read side, one-to-two-minute freshness and never-silently-failing ingestion on the write side. I'll flag now that 'trending' and 'scraping legality' both need a quick definition from you before I over-build them."

---

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

**Q1 — "Roughly how many readers are we serving, and how many sources are we ingesting from?"**
> 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "The read scale decides whether I need a CDN and a sharded cache or just one Postgres replica; the source count decides how big my ingestion fleet is."
> ↔️ Design fork:
> - If millions of DAU → CDN + sharded Redis + read replicas, cache is mandatory.
> - If thousands → a single cache + one DB replica is fine; I won't over-build.
> Assumption I'll proceed with: ~20M DAU (~1B reads/day) and ~10K sources polled — squarely "needs a real read tier."

**Q2 — "How do we define `/trending`? Most-viewed in a window, fastest-rising, or editorially curated — and is it global or per-region/per-category?"**
> 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "Trending is the one feed I can't just cache forever — its whole point is that it changes — so its definition directly drives whether I need a streaming counter."
> ↔️ Design fork:
> - If velocity-based (rising fast) → I need windowed view-event counts in a stream processor.
> - If simple most-viewed-today → a periodic batch job over a counter table is enough.
> Assumption: **velocity-based, per-category and per-region, over a sliding ~1-hour window.** That's the harder, more realistic version.

**Q3 — "How fresh must content be — is a 1–2 minute lag from publication acceptable, or do we need true real-time for breaking news?"**
> 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "Sub-minute changes everything — it forces push-based ingestion and a hotter pipeline; a couple minutes lets me poll on a comfortable schedule."
> ↔️ Design fork:
> - If seconds matter → prioritize webhooks/PubSubHubbub push feeds + a fast-lane queue.
> - If minutes are fine → scheduled polling with adaptive intervals.
> Assumption: **1–2 minute freshness target**, with a fast-lane for sources that support push.

**Q4 — "Do we store and serve the full article body, or just a headline + snippet + link out to the publisher?"**
> 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "Storing full bodies blows up storage and raises copyright questions; snippet-and-link is lighter and safer."
> ↔️ Design fork:
> - If full body → blob store (S3) for content + larger search index.
> - If snippet + deep-link → tiny rows, link out, much cheaper.
> Assumption: **store full normalized text for dedup/search/classification internally, but serve snippet + canonical link** to readers (legally safer, cheaper egress).

**Q5 — "What consistency do readers need — is it OK if a brand-new article takes a few seconds to appear in every feed, as long as it's not lost?"**
> 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "If eventual consistency is fine, I can cache aggressively and fan out asynchronously; if every reader must see an article the instant it lands, I lose most of my caching room."
> ↔️ Design fork:
> - If eventual (seconds) OK → async fan-out, heavy caching. (Much cheaper/faster.)
> - If strict → read-from-primary, tiny TTLs, expensive.
> Assumption: **eventual consistency, seconds-level**, is acceptable for a news feed — nobody's money is at stake.

**Q6 — "Is scraping in scope, and if so do we have permission / must we respect robots.txt and rate limits?"**
> 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "Scraping is a legal and operational minefield — politeness limits and ToS — and it changes my ingestion design and on-call burden."
> ↔️ Design fork:
> - If scraping allowed → per-domain polite rate-limiters + headless fetch workers.
> - If RSS/API only → simpler, no scraping fleet.
> Assumption: **RSS + public APIs are primary; scraping is a minority path behind a polite, per-domain rate limiter**, only for sources without feeds.

**Q7 — "How accurate must auto-categorization be, and is a human-moderation / correction loop in scope?"**
> 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "If 95% is fine I can use a lightweight model in-line; if we need near-perfect, I need a human-in-the-loop and re-classification pipeline, which is a whole subsystem."
> ↔️ Design fork:
> - If best-effort → in-line classifier service, async re-tag job.
> - If high-stakes (moderation/spam) → add a review queue + feedback loop.
> Assumption: **best-effort ML categorization in-line, plus an async re-classification job** when models improve; light spam filter on by default.

> 🤝 Checkpoint: "Those are my assumptions — 20M DAU, velocity-based trending, 1–2 min freshness, snippet-and-link serving, eventual consistency, RSS/API-first. If any of those is wrong, especially the trending definition, tell me now because it changes where I spend my effort. Otherwise I'll size the system."

---

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I'll compute only the few numbers that actually drive a decision, and round everything to something I can say out loud.

**Glossary first:** *QPS / RPS* = queries (requests) per second hitting the servers; *DAU* = daily active users; *TTL* = time-to-live, how long a cached value lives before it expires; *hit ratio* = fraction of reads served from cache without touching the database.

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ingest rate | 500 articles/min peak ÷ 60 = 8.3/s | ~8/s | "under ten articles a second" | Ingestion is tiny — a few workers + a queue, not a fleet |
| Unique articles/day | 720K ingested/day × ~50% dedup rate = 360K | ~360K/day | "about a third of a million new stories a day" | Storage stays small; dedup is correctness, not volume |
| Avg read QPS | 20M DAU × 50 reads/day = 1B/day ÷ 86,400s = 11,574/s | ~12K/s | "about twelve thousand reads a second" | Need a cache + replicas; no single DB serves this |
| Peak read QPS | 12K × 3 (peak multiplier) = 34,722/s | ~35K/s | "about thirty-five thousand a second at peak" | **The number that forces the whole read tier** |
| Read:write ratio | 11,574 reads ÷ 8.3 ingest = 1,389 | ~1,400:1 | "roughly fourteen hundred reads per write" | Optimize relentlessly for reads; writes are cheap |
| Cache-miss QPS | 35K peak × (1 − 0.95 hit) = 1,736/s | ~1,700/s | "under two thousand a second reach the DB" | A few read replicas absorb misses comfortably |
| Cold-cache worst case | 35K × (1 − 0) = 35K/s to origin | ~35K/s | "thirty-five thousand if cache is empty" | Need cache warming + request coalescing on cold start |
| Hot-metadata storage | 360K/day × 365 × 6KB × ~4 (indexes+3× repl+search) ≈ 3.2 TB/yr | ~3 TB/yr | "a few terabytes a year" | Storage is a non-issue; content blobs go to cheap S3 |

**The one number that forces the design: ~35K reads/sec at peak.** A single Postgres primary tops out around a few thousand simple QPS; a single Redis node tops out ~150K ops/s. So 35K is *under* one Redis node's ceiling but I'll still **shard the cache and front it with a CDN** for headroom, availability, and to keep p99 flat — and I'll size read replicas off the ~1,700/s *miss* QPS, not the full 35K. **Flip threshold:** if peak reads cross ~120–150K/s (a single hot node's ceiling), cache sharding stops being optional and becomes load-bearing.

> 🎙️ Script: "The shape here is lopsided — about twelve thousand reads a second average, thirty-five at peak, against under ten ingested articles a second. That's roughly fourteen hundred reads per write, so I'm going to spend almost all my budget on the read path: a CDN plus a cache that turns thirty-five thousand requests into under two thousand that actually hit a database. Writes and storage are basically rounding errors — a few terabytes a year — so I won't waste design time there."

---

## 4. Core Entities

This is a first draft of the nouns; I'll give full field definitions in §7.

**Article** — the central entity: one normalized news story (title, body, author, publishedAt, source, canonical URL) plus a content hash and a cluster id.
> 🗣️ Simple analogy: "Think of an Article like one clean index card per story — no matter which messy website it came from, we copy it onto the same standard card."
> ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "How do you stop the same story appearing as ten Articles?" → each Article carries a `cluster_id`; near-duplicates share a cluster and we surface one representative per cluster in feeds.

**Source** — a publisher/feed we ingest from (RSS URL or API endpoint), with poll interval, reliability stats, and last-success timestamp.
> 🗣️ Simple analogy: "Think of a Source like a subscription in a feed reader — an address we check on a schedule, remembering when we last heard from it."
> ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "How do you know a Source silently died?" → we track `last_success_at` and error rate per Source and alarm when a feed goes quiet or starts erroring (N4).

**Category & Tag** — the topic taxonomy (Politics, Sports…) and free-form labels (AI, India); many-to-many with Article.
> 🗣️ Simple analogy: "Think of Categories like the sections of a newspaper and Tags like the keywords in the index at the back — one story can be in several of each."
> ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "An article belongs to multiple categories — how do you model that?" → a join table `article_category` (and `article_tag`), not a column, so membership is many-to-many and cheap to query.

**Cluster** — a group of Articles that are the same underlying story; owns the "representative" article and an aggregate popularity score.
> 🗣️ Simple analogy: "Think of a Cluster as a folder that holds every outlet's version of one event, with the best copy pinned to the front."
> ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "Is the cluster decided at write time or read time?" → at write time (during ingestion) so reads stay cheap; the dedup deep dive covers how.

**ViewEvent** — an append-only record that someone viewed/clicked an Article; the raw fuel for `/trending` and popularity.
> 🗣️ Simple analogy: "Think of a ViewEvent like a turnstile click — we don't care who, just that one more person walked through, so we can tell what's hot."
> ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "Do you write one DB row per view?" → no — views go to an event stream and are aggregated in windows; per-view DB writes would be ~35K/s of pointless load.

> 🎙️ Script: "The nouns are Article, Source, Category and Tag, Cluster, and ViewEvent. The one people underestimate is Cluster — it's what makes dedup work, because feeds show one card per cluster, not per raw article. ViewEvent is interesting too: it never touches the primary database, it flows into a stream so trending stays cheap. I'll flesh out the exact fields, keys, and which store each lives in when I get to the data model."

---

## 5. API / Interface

REST throughout — these are cacheable, resource-shaped reads plus a small internal write path. The caller is identified from a JWT/session for personalization and rate-limiting; the client never supplies trust-sensitive fields like popularity scores.

| Method + path | Auth | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `GET /v1/latest?cursor=&limit=` | optional JWT | newest articles, reverse-chron |
| `GET /v1/trending?region=&category=&limit=` | optional JWT | currently-hot articles |
| `GET /v1/category/:name?sort=&cursor=&limit=` | optional JWT | one section |
| `GET /v1/search?q=&sort=&cursor=` | optional JWT | full-text search |
| `GET /v1/articles/:id` | optional JWT | one article detail |
| `POST /internal/ingest` | service-to-service | enqueue a raw fetched item (internal only) |
| `POST /v1/articles/:id/view` | optional JWT | record a view (fire-and-forget) |

**`GET /v1/latest?cursor=&limit=`** — GET because it's a pure, cacheable read.
- Request: `cursor: opaque string` — encodes (publishedAt, articleId) of the last item seen, so paging is **cursor-based not offset-based** (offset `OFFSET 100000` scans and skips rows — death at scale; a cursor is an indexed seek). `limit: int` capped server-side (e.g. ≤50) so a client can't request a million rows.
- Response: `items: [ArticleCard]` (one card *per cluster*, deduped), `nextCursor`.
- Key decision: **cursor pagination + dedup-by-cluster at read time is invisible to the client** — they just keep passing `nextCursor`.

**`GET /v1/trending?region=&category=`** — GET, but the *shortest* TTL of any endpoint.
- Request: `region`, `category` — both optional filters; server derives the ranking, client cannot pass a score.
- Response: `items: [ArticleCard]` ordered by a server-computed velocity score, plus `asOf` timestamp so the client can show "updated 30s ago".
- Key decision: trending is **precomputed by a streaming job and cached with a ~30–60s TTL** — the request never computes the ranking inline.

**`GET /v1/search?q=&sort=`** — GET; query in the querystring so results are CDN-cacheable for popular queries.
- Request: `q: string` (the query), `sort: recency|relevance` (default relevance). The server, not the client, decides relevance scoring.
- Response: `items` with a `highlight` snippet showing why each matched.
- Key decision: served from a **dedicated search index (Elasticsearch)**, never from the primary DB; popular queries are additionally CDN-cached briefly.

**`POST /v1/articles/:id/view`** — POST because it records new state, but **fire-and-forget**: returns `202 Accepted` immediately and pushes the event to a stream.
- Request: `articleId` (from path), caller identity from JWT; client does **not** send a timestamp or weight — the server stamps server-time so views can't be forged to game trending.
- Key decision: **idempotency via (userId, articleId, minute-bucket)** dedup key so a double-tap or client retry doesn't inflate counts; the endpoint never blocks the reader.

> 💬 "Notice the client never sends popularity, scores, or timestamps for trending — all of that is server-derived, so nobody can POST their way to the top of trending."

> 🎙️ Script: "The read API is four GETs — latest, trending, category, search — all cursor-paginated and cacheable, returning one card per *cluster* so duplicates never reach the client. The only write a reader makes is recording a view, and that's deliberately fire-and-forget: it returns immediately and drops an event on a stream, because at thirty-five thousand reads a second I can't afford a database write per view. Security-wise, anything that affects ranking — scores, timestamps — is computed server-side, never trusted from the request."

---

## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)

I'll build the four core flows in order — ingestion, categorization, reader feeds, search — starting minimal and only adding components a slice actually needs.

### 6.1 — F1: "Ingest & normalize articles from many sources (with dedup)"

The user here is *the system itself* pulling from the outside world: fetch raw items on a schedule, normalize them, drop duplicates, and hand clean Articles downstream.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Scheduler + Fetcher fleet | A cron-like scheduler that tells worker processes which Sources to poll, and workers that fetch RSS/API/scrape | We have ~10K sources on different intervals; a scheduler + horizontal workers cleanly parallelizes polite polling | Not one big cron monolith (no parallelism, one slow feed blocks all); not per-source threads (unbounded, unmanageable) | Scheduler is a coordinator we must make HA |
| Ingestion queue (Kafka) | A durable append-only log that buffers fetched items between fetchers and processors | Decouples bursty fetching from steady processing so nothing is lost if a downstream step is slow | Not direct DB writes (couples fetch speed to DB; a slow DB stalls fetching); not RabbitMQ (we want replay + multiple consumers) | One more system to operate |
| Normalizer/Dedup worker | A service that maps each raw item to our schema, computes a content hash + similarity signature, and assigns a cluster | Normalization + dedup must happen once, centrally, before anything stores or indexes the article | Not dedup at read time (would re-run on every one of 35K reads/s — absurd); not exact-hash-only (misses reworded near-dupes) | Some near-dupe tuning/false-merge risk (N5) |
| Article DB (PostgreSQL) | A relational database storing the normalized Article rows and the cluster/category join tables | Relationships (article↔cluster↔category↔tag) and ordered "latest" queries fit SQL perfectly at our modest write rate | Not a pure KV store (can't do the relational joins/ordering cheaply); not a document DB (we want strict schema + joins) | Must shard/replicate eventually (cheap at 8 writes/s) |

> 🎙️ Narration: "A scheduler hands ~10K sources to a fleet of fetcher workers that politely pull RSS, APIs, and the occasional scrape. Rather than write straight to the database — which would couple my fetch speed to my DB and lose data if the DB hiccups — every fetched item lands on Kafka, a durable log that just buffers events. A normalizer reads from Kafka, reshapes each item into our one schema, computes a fingerprint to catch duplicates, assigns a cluster, and writes the clean Article to Postgres. I considered de-duplicating at read time and rejected it instantly — I'd be re-running it on thirty-five thousand reads a second instead of eight writes."

> 🗣️ Key terms for this slice:
> **Kafka** — a durable, replayable log that holds a stream of events so producers and consumers run at their own speed. Here it buffers fetched items so a slow normalizer or DB never causes us to drop news.
> **Content hash / similarity signature** — a short fingerprint of an article's text (exact hash for identical copies, a MinHash/SimHash sketch for near-duplicates). Here it's how we detect the same story across outlets.
> **Cluster** — the group id shared by all versions of one story. Here it's assigned at ingestion so feeds can show one card per story.
> **PostgreSQL** — a relational database with tables, joins, and transactions. Here it's the source of truth for normalized articles and their category/tag relationships.

**DIAGRAM — F1: Ingestion & Dedup pipeline**

```mermaid

    RSS[RSS feeds]:::ext
    API[News APIs]:::ext
    WEB[Scraped sites]:::ext
  end

  SCH -->|"1: due sources"| FET
```

↳ reuses existing: none (this is the foundation slice).

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. **Scheduler picks due Sources.** It owns the polling calendar — each Source has its own interval (a high-volume wire every 30s, a sleepy blog hourly). *State:* the Source's `next_poll_at` advances. *What if the scheduler dies?* it's HA with a standby and the schedule is in the DB, so a restart resumes; worst case a poll is a minute late (within N3).
2. **Fetchers pull from the source.** Workers do the actual HTTP/RSS/scrape, applying a **per-domain politeness rate-limiter** for scraping. *State:* none persisted yet. *What if a source errors/times out?* the worker records the failure on the Source and retries with backoff; repeated failure alarms (N4).
3. **Raw item is appended to Kafka.** *Why:* this decouples fetching from processing and gives us replay. *State:* an immutable event on the log. *What if the normalizer is down?* items sit safely on Kafka and are processed when it recovers — nothing is lost.
4. **Normalizer consumes the item.** *Why here:* normalization/dedup is centralized so it happens exactly once. *State:* in-flight transformation.
5. **Full body to object store (S3).** *Why:* bodies are large and rarely served whole; cheap blob storage keeps the relational DB lean. *State:* body persisted, DB row will hold the S3 key + snippet.
6. **Clean Article written to Postgres with its `cluster_id`.** *Why Postgres owns it:* it's the relational source of truth. *State:* the Article now exists and is queryable; downstream categorization and indexing trigger off this write (via CDC, see 6.4). *What if it's a near-duplicate?* it's attached to the existing cluster instead of creating a new visible card (deep dive §8).

> 💬 "The key insight: I dedup *once* at write time and tag every article with a cluster — so the 35K-reads-a-second feed never has to think about duplicates again."

> 🎙️ Script: "Ingestion is a classic decoupled pipeline. A scheduler tells fetcher workers which of my ten thousand sources are due; they pull and drop raw items onto Kafka — a durable log — so nothing's lost if the downstream is slow. A normalizer turns each item into one clean schema, fingerprints it to detect the same story across outlets, assigns a cluster, parks the big body in S3, and writes a lean Article row to Postgres. The one decision that makes the whole system cheap is doing dedup here, once, at eight writes a second — never on the read path."

### 6.2 — F2: "Categorize & tag articles automatically"

Once an Article exists, we need to file it under categories and attach tags — without blocking the live pipeline, and re-runnable when models improve.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Classifier service | A stateless service wrapping an ML text classifier that returns categories + tags with confidences | Classification is CPU-bound and independently scalable; a separate service lets us swap/upgrade models without touching ingestion | Not inline in the normalizer (couples model latency to ingestion; a slow model stalls news); not a manual taxonomy (doesn't scale to 360K/day) | Eventual: an article is briefly un-categorized for a second or two |
| Classify queue (Kafka topic) | A topic carrying "new article needs classifying" events, fed by CDC off the Article DB | Async so categorization never blocks an article from appearing in `/latest`; replayable for re-classification | Not synchronous call from normalizer (blocks ingestion on model latency) | Slight tag delay; ordering not guaranteed (fine) |
| article_category / article_tag tables | Join tables expressing the many-to-many between articles and topics/labels | An article belongs to many categories/tags; join tables make "all Tech articles" a fast indexed query | Not array columns (hard to index/query both directions); not duplicating article per category | Writes touch 2-3 rows; trivial at our rate |

> 🎙️ Narration: "When a new Article lands, a change-data-capture stream drops a 'needs classifying' event on a Kafka topic. A separate classifier service — wrapping an ML model — picks it up, predicts categories and tags, and writes rows into join tables. I deliberately did NOT call the model synchronously during ingestion: model latency is unpredictable, and I never want a slow classifier to delay breaking news from showing up in /latest. The cost is that for a second or two an article might be uncategorized — totally fine for a news feed."

> 🗣️ Key terms for this slice:
> **CDC (Change Data Capture)** — a stream of every insert/update to a database table, read from its write-ahead log. Here it turns "a new Article was written" into an event other systems react to, with no dual-writes.
> **Classifier service** — a service that runs a machine-learning model to label text. Here it predicts an article's categories (Politics/Sports/Tech) and tags (AI, India).
> **Join table** — a table whose rows pair two ids (article_id, category_id) to express a many-to-many link. Here it's how one article lives in several categories at once.

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. **CDC emits a 'new article' event.** *Why:* CDC avoids dual-writes — the classifier reacts to the same commit that created the article. *State:* an event representing the insert. *What if CDC lags?* tags arrive a little later; the article is already live in /latest, so readers aren't blocked.
2. **Event enqueued on the classify topic.** *State:* durable, replayable — critical for re-classification when we ship a better model.
3. **Classifier consumes and predicts.** *Why a separate service:* model inference is CPU-heavy and scales independently. *What if the model is down?* events back up on Kafka and drain on recovery — no loss, just delay.
4. **Category/tag join rows written.** *State:* the article now appears under `/category/Sports`, etc. *What if confidence is low?* we attach a provisional/"uncategorized" bucket and let the async re-tag job revisit.
5. **Article indexed into Elasticsearch with its tags.** *State:* now searchable and filterable by tag (sets up §6.4). *What if indexing fails?* retried from the same event; ES lagging the DB by seconds is acceptable.

> 💬 "I keep categorization off the critical path on purpose — breaking news shows up *now*, and its labels catch up a second later."

> 🎙️ Script: "Categorization is fully asynchronous. Change-data-capture turns every new article into an event; a separate classifier service consumes it, runs the model, and writes many-to-many category and tag rows, then indexes the article for search. The reason it's async is freshness: I will never let an unpredictable ML model delay a breaking story from hitting the feed. And because the events live on Kafka, when we train a better model we just replay the topic to re-tag the whole archive."

### 6.3 — F3: "Serve reader feeds: /latest, /trending, /category, sorted"

This is the hot path — ~35K reads/sec. The reader wants a fast, ranked, deduped list. Start minimal (DB read) and earn the cache.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CDN / edge cache | Geographically distributed caches that serve responses close to readers | `/latest` and `/category` change slowly (seconds); a CDN absorbs the bulk of 35K/s with a tiny TTL | Not origin-only (35K/s of identical feed requests to our servers is wasteful) | Up to TTL seconds of staleness (fine, N5) |
| Feed/API service | Stateless service that assembles a feed page (dedup-by-cluster, paginate) | A thin, scalable layer to apply per-user filters and cursoring on cached data | Not serving straight from CDN with no logic (can't personalize/paginate) | One more hop; stateless so trivially scaled |
| Redis (feed cache) | An in-memory store holding precomputed feed pages and trending lists | Sub-millisecond reads; turns ~35K/s into ~1.7K/s of DB misses | Not Memcached (no sorted sets for trending/ranked lists); not DB-only (can't take 35K/s) | Cache invalidation/TTL tuning |
| Read replicas (Postgres) | Read-only copies of the Article DB that serve cache misses | Spread the ~1.7K/s miss load and isolate reads from the write primary | Not single primary for reads (couples read load to the write node) | Replica lag of a few seconds (acceptable) |
| Trending stream processor | A streaming job (Flink/Kafka Streams) counting ViewEvents in sliding windows | `/trending` is velocity-based and can't be a static cache; windowed counts are exactly this | Not a periodic SQL `COUNT` (too slow/stale, hammers DB); not per-view DB writes (35K/s) | Approximate counts; windowing complexity |

> 🎙️ Narration: "The read path is layered for cheapness. A CDN at the edge serves most feed requests within a few seconds of staleness. Behind it, a stateless feed service assembles the page — deduping by cluster, applying the cursor — reading precomputed lists from Redis, an in-memory store. On a cache miss it hits a Postgres read replica, not the primary. Trending is the special case: it can't be a frozen cache because its whole point is change, so a stream processor counts view events in sliding windows and writes the ranked list into Redis every thirty seconds. I picked Redis specifically for its sorted sets, which give me ranked lists for free."

> 🗣️ Key terms for this slice:
> **CDN (Content Delivery Network)** — a network of edge servers that cache responses near users. Here it absorbs most of the 35K/s identical feed reads so our origin barely sees them.
> **Redis** — an in-memory key/value store with rich types like sorted sets. Here it holds precomputed feed pages and the ranked trending list for sub-millisecond reads.
> **Read replica** — a read-only streaming copy of the database. Here it serves the ~1.7K/s of cache misses so the write primary stays unloaded.
> **Stream processor (Flink/Kafka Streams)** — a system that does continuous computation over an event stream, e.g. counts in time windows. Here it computes trending velocity from ViewEvents.
> **Sorted set** — a Redis structure that keeps members ordered by a score. Here it *is* the trending leaderboard, updated as counts change.

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. **Reader requests a feed.** Hits the nearest CDN edge first. *State:* none. *What if it's a personalized feed?* personalized pages skip the shared CDN cache and go to the service with a per-user cache key.
2. **CDN miss → feed service.** *Why:* only misses (a few %) reach us; the CDN eats the rest. *State:* none yet.
3. **Service reads from Redis.** Precomputed `/latest` page or trending sorted set. *Why Redis:* sub-ms, ranked lists native. *What if Redis is down?* fall back to a read replica and serve slightly slower — degraded, not down (N1).
4. **Redis miss → Postgres read replica.** *Why replica:* isolates reads from the write primary. *State:* result is written back into Redis for the next reader (cache-aside). *What if all replicas lag?* readers see data a few seconds stale — acceptable per N5.
5. **Reader records a view (202).** Fire-and-forget to Kafka. *State:* one view event appended. *What if the client retries?* idempotency key `(user, article, minute)` dedups it (§5).
6. **Stream processor counts in windows.** *Why streaming:* trending is velocity; windows give "rising in the last hour". *State:* running per-article counts.
7. **Ranked list written to Redis.** *State:* `/trending` reflects the latest ~30–60s. *What if the processor falls behind?* trending is slightly stale — acceptable; it never blocks reads.

> 💬 "The whole read tier is one idea: turn 35,000 requests a second into under 2,000 the database ever sees — and make trending the *only* thing that's allowed to be dynamic, because it has to be."

> 🎙️ Script: "Reads are a layered cache. The CDN serves most feed requests at the edge with a few seconds' staleness; a stateless feed service handles the misses, reading precomputed pages and the trending leaderboard from Redis, and only falling through to a Postgres read replica on a true miss — about seventeen hundred a second out of thirty-five thousand. Trending is the one thing I can't freeze, so view events flow to a stream processor that counts them in sliding windows and refreshes a Redis sorted set every thirty seconds. If Redis dies, I degrade to the replica rather than going down."

### 6.4 — F4: "Full-text search: /search?q=, ranked by relevance"

The reader types free text; we return ranked matches across the whole archive in <400ms. SQL `LIKE` can't do this — we need an inverted index.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Search index (Elasticsearch) | A search engine that pre-builds a word→articles map and ranks matches by relevance | We need ranked full-text search over 100M+ articles in <400ms; relevance scoring is built in | Not SQL `LIKE`/full-text (scans rows, weak ranking, slow at 100M); not a hand-rolled index (months of work, no ranking) | Lags the DB by seconds; eventual consistency |
| Indexer (from CDC) | The classifier/indexer step that writes each article + tags into ES | Reuses the same CDC event that drives categorization — index and tags stay in sync | Not dual-writes from the app (risks DB/ES divergence on partial failure) | Index lag of seconds |
| Query cache (CDN/Redis) | Brief caching of popular query results | A few queries (today's big story) dominate; caching them cuts ES load | Not caching everything (long-tail queries are unique) | Stale for the TTL on hot queries |

> 🎙️ Narration: "Search runs entirely off Elasticsearch — a search engine that pre-builds an inverted index, a word-to-articles map, so a query returns ranked matches in milliseconds instead of scanning a hundred million rows. Articles get indexed by the same CDC-driven step that categorizes them, so I don't dual-write and risk the DB and index drifting apart. Popular queries — everyone searching the same breaking story — get briefly cached so I don't pound Elasticsearch with identical work."

> 🗣️ Key terms for this slice:
> **Elasticsearch** — a distributed search engine built on an inverted index with relevance ranking (BM25). Here it's the only thing that can do ranked full-text search across the archive fast.
> **Inverted index** — a precomputed map from each word to the list of documents containing it. Here it's why search is milliseconds, not a full-table scan.
> **BM25 / relevance score** — a standard formula ranking how well a document matches a query (term frequency, rarity). Here it orders search results by relevance.

> 🎙️ Script: "Search is Elasticsearch, full stop — it pre-builds a word-to-document index and ranks with BM25, so I get sub-second ranked results over the whole archive, which no SQL query can. I keep it fed through the same change-data-capture stream that categorizes articles, so there's a single sync path and no dual-write drift. Hot queries are briefly cached at the edge. If ES degrades, I fall back to a plain recency list so search still returns something."

## 7. Data Model & Storage

**PART A — ENTITY TABLE**

| Entity | Key fields (PK / shard key annotated) | Chosen store | Shard / partition key | Consistency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Article | **id (PK)**, cluster_id, source_id, title, snippet, body_s3_key, author, published_at, url, content_hash, simhash | Postgres (+ S3 for body) | hash(id); time-partitioned by published_at | Strong within row; replicas eventual (secs) |
| Source | **id (PK)**, type(rss/api/scrape), url, poll_interval, last_success_at, error_rate | Postgres | id | Strong |
| Cluster | **id (PK)**, representative_article_id, story_count, popularity_score, first_seen_at | Postgres | id | Strong on membership; score eventual |
| Category | **id (PK)**, name | Postgres | id (small, replicated everywhere) | Strong |
| Tag | **id (PK)**, name | Postgres | id | Strong |
| article_category | **(article_id, category_id) PK** | Postgres | article_id | Strong |
| article_tag | **(article_id, tag_id) PK** | Postgres | article_id | Strong |
| Search doc | id, title, body, tags[], published_at | Elasticsearch | routing by id | Eventual (secs behind PG) |
| Feed page / trending | key→list, sorted sets | Redis | key | Eventual (TTL/window) |
| ViewEvent | article_id, user_id, ts, minute_bucket | Kafka → aggregates | partition by article_id | At-least-once; deduped in window |

**PART B — STORAGE DECISION CARDS**

**🗄️ PostgreSQL — used for: Article, Source, Cluster, Category/Tag + join tables (source of truth)**
1. What it is: a relational database — data in typed rows and columns with strict schemas, joins, and transactions that fully succeed or fully roll back.
2. Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces it: our data is deeply *relational* (article↔cluster↔category↔tag, source↔article) and our core reads are "newest N in a category" — ordered, joined queries. At ~8 writes/s the write load is trivial, so SQL's relational power costs us nothing and buys clean integrity.
3. What we considered instead:
   - **DynamoDB / pure KV** — fast for single-key gets, but expressing "all Tech articles newest-first" across a many-to-many tag relationship needs ugly secondary-index gymnastics; relational joins are native in Postgres.
   - **MongoDB (document)** — flexible, but we want strict schema for normalized articles and easy joins for the taxonomy; we'd reimplement joins in app code.
   - **Cassandra** — superb write throughput we don't need (8/s) and painful for ad-hoc relational queries.
4. Trade-off we accept: we'll eventually shard/partition (by `published_at` time + hash) and manage replicas — but at this write rate that's far off and easy.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Articles and their taxonomy are relational and the write rate is tiny, so Postgres is the obvious source of truth. I looked at Dynamo — great for key lookups — but 'all Tech stories, newest first' across a many-to-many tag link is exactly what SQL joins do well and KV does badly. I time-partition by date so old news ages out cheaply."

**🗄️ Redis — used for: feed pages, trending leaderboard, dedup/idempotency keys**
1. What it is: an in-memory key/value store with rich types (sorted sets, hashes) and sub-millisecond latency.
2. Why we picked it — the access pattern: the read tier must turn 35K/s into a trickle of DB misses, and trending needs a *ranked* structure updated continuously — Redis sorted sets are a leaderboard out of the box.
3. What we considered instead:
   - **Memcached** — simple cache, but no sorted sets, so I'd rebuild ranking in app code for trending.
   - **DB-only with query cache** — can't absorb 35K/s; the DB becomes the bottleneck.
4. Trade-off we accept: cache invalidation and TTL tuning, plus memory cost; data is derived/rebuildable so loss isn't catastrophic.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Redis is my read accelerator and my trending leaderboard in one — sorted sets give me ranked lists for free, which Memcached can't. It's all derived data, so if a node dies I just rebuild from Postgres and the stream."

**🗄️ Elasticsearch — used for: full-text search**
1. What it is: a distributed search engine built on an inverted index with relevance ranking.
2. Why we picked it — the access pattern: ranked full-text search across 100M+ articles in <400ms is impossible with SQL `LIKE` (full scans, no ranking); ES is purpose-built for it.
3. What we considered instead:
   - **Postgres full-text** — fine for thousands of docs, not 100M with low latency and good ranking.
   - **Hand-rolled inverted index** — months of work and we'd still lack mature ranking/sharding.
4. Trade-off we accept: it's a second copy of the data that lags the DB by seconds (eventual) and costs RAM/CPU.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Search is its own problem — I need ranked matches over a hundred million articles in well under a second, which only a real search engine does. ES is a derived copy kept in sync by CDC, so it can lag the database by a few seconds and nobody notices."

**🗄️ Kafka — used for: raw-items log, classify/index topic, view-events**
1. What it is: a durable, partitioned, replayable log of events.
2. Why we picked it — the access pattern: it decouples bursty ingestion and fire-hose view events from steady processors, and its replay lets us re-classify/re-index the whole archive when models improve.
3. What we considered instead:
   - **RabbitMQ / SQS** — good queues, but no long-retention replay across multiple independent consumers, which we rely on.
   - **Direct service calls** — couple components and lose buffering, so one slow consumer drops news.
4. Trade-off we accept: an extra distributed system to operate and reason about ordering.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Kafka is the spine connecting the two halves — it buffers ingestion and view events so nothing's lost, and because it's replayable I can re-tag or re-index everything just by replaying a topic."

**🗄️ S3 (object store) — used for: full article bodies**
1. What it is: cheap, durable blob storage for large opaque objects.
2. Why we picked it: bodies are large and rarely served whole; keeping them out of Postgres keeps rows lean and reads fast.
3. What we considered instead: **bodies in Postgres** (bloats the DB, slows everything); **bodies in ES only** (it's an index, not a store of record).
4. Trade-off: an extra fetch when we do need the full body.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Big article bodies go to S3 with the key stored on the row — eleven-nines durable, dirt cheap, and it keeps my hot database small."

**PART C — Per-operation consistency summary**

| Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why that level is right |
|---|---|---|---|
| Write/cluster a new Article | Postgres primary | Strong | Source of truth; cluster membership must be exact |
| Read `/latest`, `/category` | Redis → replica | Eventual (secs) | News tolerates seconds of lag; enables heavy caching |
| Read `/trending` | Redis (windowed) | Eventual/approximate | Velocity is inherently approximate; ~30–60s fresh is fine |
| Search | Elasticsearch | Eventual (secs) | Index lag of seconds is invisible to readers |
| Record a view | Kafka → aggregates | At-least-once, deduped | Exactness not needed; dedup key prevents double-count |
| Editor/source config change | Postgres primary | Strong, read-from-primary | Operators must see their own change immediately |

> 🎙️ Script: "Postgres is the relational source of truth for articles and the taxonomy, and at eight writes a second I never sweat its write load. Everything readers touch is a *derived, eventually-consistent* copy: Redis for fast feeds and the trending leaderboard, Elasticsearch for ranked search, both kept in sync off a single change-data-capture stream so I'm not dual-writing. Big bodies live in S3 to keep rows lean. I considered DynamoDB for articles and rejected it — 'all Tech stories newest-first' across a many-to-many tag link is a SQL join's home turf and a KV store's nightmare. The only thing I keep strictly consistent is the write of the article itself; everything downstream is allowed to be a few seconds behind, and that's what makes the read path cheap."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

> 🆘 If you get stuck: go back to "two halves" — an ingestion/correctness pipeline and a read/latency tier joined by Kafka — and reason forward from whichever bottleneck the interviewer pokes.

### How do we de-duplicate the same story across hundreds of outlets?

**#### Bad: Exact-hash dedup**
> 🗣️ What this is in plain words: "Hash the article text; if we've seen that exact hash, it's a duplicate."
Approach: compute a SHA-256 of the normalized body; keep hashes in a set; drop on collision.
Why people try this: it's trivial and catches verbatim reposts.
> ⚠️ What breaks: outlets *rewrite* wire stories — change the headline, reorder a sentence, add a byline — so the exact hash differs and the "same" story slips through as a new Article. A reader sees the Reuters story ten times. It catches maybe 20% of real duplicates.
> 🔁 What forces the upgrade: near-duplicates with tiny edits are the *common* case, not the exception.

**#### Good: Near-duplicate via MinHash/SimHash + LSH**
> ↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: exact hashing only catches byte-identical copies; it's blind to reworded versions, which are most duplicates.
> 🗣️ What this is in plain words: "Make a fuzzy fingerprint of each article so that two articles that are *mostly* the same get *similar* fingerprints, then quickly find articles with nearby fingerprints."
Approach: compute a **SimHash** (a fingerprint where similar texts produce fingerprints differing in only a few bits) or **MinHash** (a sketch estimating overlap of word-sets) per article. Use **LSH — Locality-Sensitive Hashing** (a bucketing trick that puts similar fingerprints in the same bucket) so we only compare an incoming article against a handful of candidates, not all 100M. If similarity exceeds a threshold, attach to the existing cluster.
Why this seemed reasonable: it directly models "near", and LSH makes the search sub-linear.
> ⚠️ What breaks: a single global threshold is brittle — too loose and we *over-merge* (two different election stories become one, violating N5's no-false-merge); too tight and reworded dupes leak. And ingestion is concurrent: two workers can process two copies of the same story at the same instant and both create a new cluster (a race).
> 🔁 What forces the upgrade: concurrency races + threshold brittleness need coordination and signals beyond raw text.

**#### Great: LSH candidate-gen + multi-signal scoring + atomic cluster claim**
> ↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: Good could double-create clusters under concurrency and mis-merged on text alone.
> 🗣️ What this is in plain words: "Use the fuzzy fingerprint only to *find candidates*, then decide with several signals together — text similarity, publish time, named entities, and source — and make 'join or create a cluster' an atomic, race-proof step."
Approach:
1. **Candidate generation:** LSH buckets give ~tens of candidate articles in O(1).
2. **Multi-signal scoring:** combine SimHash similarity + time proximity (same story clusters within hours) + entity overlap (same people/places via the classifier) + URL/canonical hints. Require agreement across signals before merging — kills most false merges.
3. **Atomic cluster claim:** dedup on a **per-signature lock** — `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT` on a `signature → cluster_id` table (or a Redis `SETNX` on the signature). The first writer creates the cluster; concurrent copies see the conflict and join it. This makes clustering idempotent under concurrency.
4. **Async re-clustering:** a background job revisits borderline clusters as more copies arrive, so early mistakes self-heal.

> 🔢 Decision-forcing math: comparing each of 360K new articles/day against all 100M would be 3.6×10^13 comparisons — impossible. LSH cuts it to ~tens of candidates each → ~360K × ~30 ≈ 11M cheap comparisons/day, trivially handled by a few workers.

> ✅ Failure matrix:
> | Scenario | What happens | Mitigation |
> |---|---|---|
> | Two copies processed concurrently | Both try to create a cluster | `ON CONFLICT`/`SETNX` on signature → one wins, other joins |
> | Reworded duplicate | Different exact hash, similar SimHash | LSH finds it; multi-signal confirms; merged |
> | Two distinct stories, similar wording | High text sim but different entities/time | Multi-signal disagreement → kept separate (no false merge) |
> | Signature store down | Can't claim cluster | Fail safe: create new cluster, async re-cluster job merges later |
> | Late-arriving copy after cluster sealed | New small cluster | Re-clustering job folds it into the canonical cluster |

> 🎙️ Script: "Dedup is the heart of the write side. Exact hashing is useless because outlets rewrite wire copy, so I fingerprint each article with SimHash — similar text gives similar fingerprints — and use locality-sensitive hashing to find a handful of candidates instead of comparing against a hundred million. But text alone over-merges, so I confirm with several signals together: time proximity, shared named entities, and source. The subtle bug is concurrency — two copies arriving at once both trying to start a cluster — so I make 'create-or-join' atomic with an insert-on-conflict on the signature. First one creates, the rest join. A background job re-clusters borderline cases so early calls self-heal."
> 🧠 If they ask "why not just cluster at read time?": "Because I'd be re-running similarity on thirty-five thousand reads a second instead of eight writes — clustering at write time is a thousand-times cheaper and the reader just sees one card per cluster."

### How do we compute `/trending` at 35K reads/sec without melting the DB?

**#### Bad: COUNT(*) over a views table on each request**
> 🗣️ Plain words: "Write every view to a table and count them when someone asks for trending."
Approach: a `views(article_id, ts)` row per view; `/trending` runs `SELECT article_id, COUNT(*) ... GROUP BY ... ORDER BY` over a recent window.
Why people try this: it's the obvious, correct-looking SQL.
> ⚠️ What breaks: 35K view-writes/s hammer the DB, and the GROUP BY scans millions of rows per trending request — at thousands of trending QPS the DB dies twice over.
> 🔁 What forces the upgrade: both the write rate and the aggregation cost are unsustainable.

**#### Good: Stream view-events + windowed counters in Redis**
> ↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: per-view DB writes and on-demand GROUP BY are both far too expensive.
> 🗣️ Plain words: "Don't store every view in the database — stream them, count them in rolling time-windows, and keep a ready-made leaderboard in memory."
Approach: views go to Kafka (fire-and-forget, §5). A **stream processor** (Flink/Kafka Streams) maintains **sliding-window counts** per article (e.g. last 60 min) and writes a Redis **sorted set** ranked by a velocity score. `/trending` is then an O(log n) `ZREVRANGE` read from Redis — no DB touch.
Why this works: trending becomes a cheap memory read; the DB never sees views.
> ⚠️ What breaks: a few mega-stories (breaking news) become **hot keys** — one article's counter takes a huge share of view traffic, hot-spotting a single partition/Redis key; and a naive count favors old viral stories over genuinely *rising* ones.
> 🔁 What forces the upgrade: hot-key skew + "rising vs popular" need partitioning and a velocity model.

**#### Great: Sharded counters + decay/velocity score + approximate counting**
> ↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: a single counter per hot article is a single-shard hotspot, and raw counts aren't "trending".
> 🗣️ Plain words: "Spread each hot story's counter across several shards so no single key melts, score by *rate of rise* not total, and use approximate counters since exact view counts don't matter."
Approach:
1. **Sharded counters:** key a hot article's counter as `article_id:shard_k` across N shards; sum on read. Spreads write load off any single partition.
2. **Velocity score with time-decay:** score = recent-window rate with an exponential decay, so a story doing 1K views/min *now* beats one that did 10K an hour ago. This is what makes it "trending", not "all-time popular".
3. **Approximate counting:** view counts feed ranking, not billing — use probabilistic counters / sampling under extreme load to bound cost.
4. **Per-region/category leaderboards:** maintain separate sorted sets so `/trending?region=IN&category=Sports` is a direct read.

> 🔢 Decision-forcing math: at 35K views/s a single Redis key/partition (~100K ops/s ceiling) survives *average* load, but a single breaking story can pull 50%+ of traffic — ~17K/s on one key, climbing toward the ceiling during a spike. Sharding the counter across ~16 shards drops it to ~1K/s each — comfortable.

> ✅ Failure matrix:
> | Scenario | What happens | Mitigation |
> |---|---|---|
> | Breaking-news hot key | One counter saturates | Sharded counters spread the write |
> | Stream processor lag/crash | Trending goes stale | Serve last good leaderboard; reprocess from Kafka (replayable) |
> | Redis node loss | Leaderboard gone | Rebuild from the stream in seconds; degrade to recency meanwhile |
> | View-event flood (bot spam) | Inflated counts | Idempotency key + per-user/IP rate caps + spam filter |
> | Old viral story stuck on top | Stale trending | Time-decay velocity score ages it out |

> 🎙️ Script: "Trending can't touch the database. View events stream to Kafka and a stream processor keeps sliding-window counts, writing a ranked Redis sorted set that /trending reads in log-time. The two hard parts are hot keys and the definition of 'trending'. A breaking story can pull half of all views onto one counter, so I shard that counter across, say, sixteen keys and sum them. And I score by *velocity with time-decay* — rate of rise, not all-time total — so genuinely breaking stories surface and yesterday's viral hit ages out. Counts are approximate on purpose; this feeds ranking, not a bill."
> 🧠 If they ask "how do you stop bots gaming trending?": "View events carry an idempotency key of user-article-minute and we rate-cap per user and IP, so a bot can't inflate a story by spamming taps — plus a spam filter on the event stream."

### How do we keep the read path fast and available at 35K/s (and on cold cache)?

**#### Good: CDN + Redis cache-aside + read replicas**
> 🗣️ Plain words: "Serve most reads from edge caches and memory; only true misses reach a read-only copy of the database."
Approach: CDN at the edge (seconds TTL) for shared feeds; Redis cache-aside for assembled pages; Postgres read replicas for misses. Sizing off **miss** QPS (~1.7K/s), not the full 35K.
> ⚠️ What breaks: a **cache stampede** — when a hot feed key expires, thousands of concurrent requests all miss and slam the DB at once (a "thundering herd"); and a cold cache (deploy/restart) momentarily exposes the full 35K/s to origin.
> 🔁 What forces the upgrade: synchronized expiry + cold starts need coalescing and warming.

**#### Great: Request coalescing + jittered TTL + cache warming**
> ↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: naive cache-aside lets a key's expiry trigger a herd of identical DB queries.
> 🗣️ Plain words: "When many people miss the same thing at once, let *one* request fetch it and everyone else wait for that single result; and never let everything expire at the same instant."
Approach:
1. **Single-flight / request coalescing:** on a miss for key K, the first request fetches and populates; concurrent requests for K wait on that one in-flight fetch instead of each hitting the DB.
2. **Jittered TTLs:** add randomness to expiry so keys don't all expire together (avoids synchronized stampedes).
3. **Cache warming on deploy:** pre-populate hot feeds (latest, top categories, trending) before taking traffic, so a cold start never exposes the full 35K/s.
4. **Graceful degradation:** if Redis is down, serve from replicas; if replicas lag, serve slightly stale — degraded, never down (N1).

> 🔢 Decision-forcing math: without coalescing, one hot `/latest` key expiring under 35K/s peak sends a burst of up to ~35K identical queries at the DB in the expiry instant — replicas (a few K QPS each) collapse. Coalescing turns that into **1** DB query; the rest wait ~1ms on the in-flight result.

> ✅ Failure matrix:
> | Scenario | What happens | Mitigation |
> |---|---|---|
> | Hot key expiry | Stampede to DB | Single-flight coalescing → 1 query |
> | Synchronized expiry | Many keys herd at once | Jittered TTLs |
> | Cold cache after deploy | Full load to origin | Warm cache before serving traffic |
> | Redis cluster down | Misses everywhere | Degrade to read replicas; recency-only |
> | Replica lag spike | Stale reads | Acceptable per N5; alarm if lag > threshold |

> 🎙️ Script: "The read path is a layered cache sized off the *miss* rate, not the headline thirty-five thousand. The classic trap is a cache stampede: when a hot feed key expires, every request misses at once and buries the database. I fix that with single-flight coalescing — the first miss fetches, everyone else waits on that one in-flight result — plus jittered TTLs so keys don't expire in lockstep, and I warm the cache on deploy so a cold start never exposes full load. If Redis dies, I fall back to replicas and serve slightly stale rather than going down."
> 🧠 If they ask "what's your real worst case?": "Cold cache with Redis empty — thirty-five thousand a second at the origin. That's exactly why I warm hot keys before taking traffic and coalesce misses; the DB only ever sees one query per hot key."

### How do we make ingestion reliable — never silently dropping a source? (N4)

**#### Great: Per-source health tracking + retry/backoff + dead-letter + alarms**
> 🗣️ Plain words: "Track every source's last success, retry failures with growing delays, quarantine the truly broken ones, and page a human when a publisher goes quiet."
Approach: each Source row carries `last_success_at` and a rolling error rate. Fetch failures retry with **exponential backoff** (wait longer each attempt); persistently failing items go to a **dead-letter queue (DLQ)** — a side queue for messages we couldn't process — for inspection rather than being lost. A monitor alarms when a Source hasn't succeeded within N intervals (silent-death detection) or when DLQ depth grows. Idempotent ingestion (dedup by source+url+hash) means safe retries never create duplicates.

> ✅ Failure matrix:
> | Scenario | What happens | Mitigation |
> |---|---|---|
> | Source feed times out | Fetch fails | Retry with backoff; alarm if sustained |
> | Source silently stops publishing | No new items | `last_success_at` staleness alarm |
> | Malformed/poison item | Normalizer can't parse | Route to DLQ; alarm; never blocks the stream |
> | Retry storm | Duplicate fetches | Idempotent dedup key makes retries safe |
> | Scraper hits rate limit | 429s | Per-domain limiter backs off politely |

> 🎙️ Script: "Reliability of ingestion is an operations problem, which at Amazon I'd treat as first-class. Every source tracks its last success and error rate; failures retry with exponential backoff, and anything we genuinely can't process goes to a dead-letter queue instead of vanishing. The killer requirement is *silent* failure, so I alarm when a source simply goes quiet past its expected interval — that catches a feed that broke on a Friday night before a human would. And because ingestion is idempotent on source-plus-URL-plus-hash, retries never create duplicates."
> 🧠 If they ask "how do you detect a source that changed its format silently?": "Parse-success rate per source is a tracked metric; a sudden drop alarms, and the failing items sit in the DLQ with samples so we can fix the parser."

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

**9A — Availability targets (what the "nines" mean)**
- **Read/feed path: four nines (99.99%) ≈ under 52 minutes of downtime/year.** Achieved with a CDN, multi-AZ stateless services, Redis with replicas, and Postgres read replicas. **Multi-AZ** = the service runs in several data centres within a region, so if one building loses power another takes over automatically.
- **Ingestion path: three nines (99.9%) ≈ ~8.7 hours/year is acceptable** — Kafka buffers during a blip, so a short outage delays freshness but loses nothing (it's replayable). Ingestion being briefly down is invisible to readers as long as it catches up.
> 🗣️ "I spend my availability budget where readers feel it — the feed — and let ingestion be a bit less hot because Kafka makes a short outage a delay, not a loss."

**9B — Per-dependency failure table**

| Component | What breaks if it's down | Graceful degradation (what the USER sees) | Recovery action |
|---|---|---|---|
| CDN | Edge cache misses | Slightly slower feeds; origin handles it | Multi-CDN/failover; origin scales |
| Redis (feed/trending) | No fast cache, no leaderboard | Feeds slower from replicas; trending shows last-known or recency | Rebuild from Postgres + stream (seconds) |
| Postgres primary | No new writes | Reading still works (replicas); ingestion buffers on Kafka | Failover to standby (multi-AZ) |
| Read replica | Less read capacity | Other replicas absorb; maybe slightly slower | Auto-replace replica |
| Elasticsearch | Search degraded | Search falls back to recency list from Redis/replica | Rebuild index from CDC/Postgres |
| Kafka | Pipeline stalls | Readers unaffected; freshness pauses then catches up | Multi-broker replication; resume consumers |
| Classifier service | New articles untagged briefly | Articles appear in /latest, categories lag | Drains backlog from topic on recovery |
| Stream processor | Trending stale | Show last good leaderboard | Reprocess window from Kafka |

**9C — RPO / RTO per data class**
- **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** = how much data we can lose if it crashes *right now*. **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** = how long until service is back.

| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Articles + taxonomy (Postgres) | ≈ 0 (sync replica) to seconds | < 1 min (multi-AZ failover) | Synchronous standby + PITR backups |
| Article bodies (S3) | ≈ 0 | ≈ 0 | S3 11-nines durability, versioned |
| Search index (ES) | minutes (rebuildable) | minutes | Rebuild from Postgres via CDC |
| Feed/trending (Redis) | seconds (derived) | seconds | Rebuild from DB + stream |
| View events (Kafka) | ≈ 0 within retention | seconds | Replicated brokers, replay |

> 🗣️ "Anything that's the source of truth — articles — I protect to near-zero data loss with a synchronous standby. Everything derived — search, cache, trending — I let lose data freely because I can rebuild it from the source in seconds to minutes."

**9D — Cost breakdown**
Top 3 drivers, in order:
1. **CDN/egress + read serving** — at ~1B reads/day, the bytes served to readers dominate; this is the biggest line item, not the database. *Cut it:* aggressive edge caching, snippet-not-body payloads, image/thumbnail optimization.
2. **Elasticsearch cluster** — full-text indexing of 100M+ docs needs lots of RAM/CPU; the second-biggest cost. *Cut it:* tier old articles to cheaper cold indices; index snippets, not full bodies.
3. **Kafka + stream processing** — durable retention + always-on stream jobs. *Cut it:* right-size retention; sample view events under extreme load.
> Postgres and S3 are comparatively cheap (a few TB). **The single highest-leverage optimization: push read traffic to the CDN/cache** so origin, ES, and DB barely scale with reader growth.

> 🎙️ Script: "When something fails, a reader almost never sees an outage — they see *slightly stale or slightly slower*. Redis down? Feeds come from replicas. Elasticsearch down? Search falls back to a recency list. Postgres primary down? Reads keep working off replicas and ingestion just buffers on Kafka until the standby takes over in under a minute. The only data I guard to near-zero loss is the articles themselves; search, cache, and trending are all rebuildable, so I let them be cheap and disposable."

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

**🔀 Decision: Eventual consistency on the read path vs strong consistency**
1. What we chose & why: derived caches (Redis/ES) lag the DB by seconds — it's what lets us serve 35K/s cheaply.
2. What we gave up: a brand-new article can be missing from a feed for a few seconds.
3. 🗣️ Plain words: "We trade a few seconds of 'is it there yet' for a feed that's fast and cheap for everyone."
4. When this reverses: if a use case demanded read-your-own-writes instantly (e.g. an editor must see their just-published piece live), I'd read that path from the primary with a version token.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "For a news feed, seconds of staleness is invisible and buys me a thousand-fold cheaper read path — easy trade. If editors needed instant confirmation, I'd route just their read to the primary."

**🔀 Decision: Dedup/cluster at write time vs read time**
1. What we chose & why: cluster once at ingestion (~8/s) so reads never recompute.
2. What we gave up: more complex ingestion, and early clustering mistakes need an async re-cluster job to heal.
3. 🗣️ Plain words: "We do the hard de-duplication work once when a story arrives, not every time someone scrolls."
4. When this reverses: if articles were rarely read but constantly re-evaluated, or if clustering rules changed per-user, read-time would win.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Clustering at write time is a thousand times cheaper than at read time here — I just need a background job to fix the occasional early misjudgment."

**🔀 Decision: Async categorization/indexing (CDC) vs synchronous at ingestion**
1. What we chose & why: tag/index off a CDC event so a slow model never blocks an article from appearing.
2. What we gave up: an article is briefly live but untagged/unsearchable (a second or two).
3. 🗣️ Plain words: "We let breaking news show up instantly and let its labels catch up a beat later."
4. When this reverses: if categories were a hard gate (e.g. legal must classify before publish), I'd make it synchronous and eat the latency.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Freshness beats completeness for news, so I never let the classifier sit on the critical path — CDC lets tagging happen just behind the live article."

**🔀 Decision: Postgres source-of-truth vs a NoSQL-first design**
1. What we chose & why: the data is relational and writes are tiny (8/s), so SQL's joins/integrity are free value.
2. What we gave up: eventual sharding effort; not "web-scale writes" out of the box (which we don't need).
3. 🗣️ Plain words: "Our data is full of relationships and we barely write, so a classic relational database is the simple right answer."
4. When this reverses: if write rate exploded (e.g. we stored every view as a row, or ingestion hit 100K/s), I'd move hot tables to Cassandra/partitioned stores.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "People reach for NoSQL reflexively, but I write only eight articles a second across a richly relational model — Postgres is the boring correct choice, and I shard later if I ever need to."

> 🎙️ Script: "My least-certain calls are all on the consistency axis. I chose eventual consistency everywhere readers touch, which makes the read path cheap but means a new article can lag a few seconds — I'd only flip it for editor read-your-own-writes. I dedup at write time because reads outnumber writes a thousand to one. I tag and index asynchronously so the model never delays breaking news. And I keep Postgres as the source of truth because the data's relational and the write rate is trivial — the thing that would reverse that is a write-rate explosion, at which point hot tables move to a partitioned store."
