## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**❓ "Two outlets run the same wire story seconds apart — how do you make sure readers see it once?"**
The mechanism: at ingestion each article gets a SimHash fingerprint; LSH finds near-duplicate candidates; a multi-signal check (text + time + entities) confirms, and an atomic `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT` on the signature makes "create-or-join cluster" race-proof, so concurrent copies join one cluster. Feeds render one card per cluster.
> 🗣️ In plain words: "We fingerprint each story so similar ones match, group them into one folder, and always show the folder, not every copy."
> 💬 "Same story across outlets collapses into one cluster at write time, so the feed shows it exactly once."

**❓ "What happens when Redis goes down during peak traffic?"**
The mechanism: the feed service degrades to Postgres read replicas for pages and serves recency in place of the trending leaderboard; single-flight coalescing prevents a stampede while the cache is cold; Redis rebuilds from the DB and the stream within seconds.
> 🗣️ In plain words: "Feeds get a bit slower and trending falls back to 'newest', but nothing goes down, and the cache refills fast."
> 💬 "Redis is derived data — lose it and I degrade to replicas, I never go dark."

**❓ "Election night — traffic 5×'s and everyone's on the same three stories. Does it hold?"**
The mechanism: CDN absorbs identical feed reads at the edge; hot trending counters are sharded across ~16 keys so no single Redis key saturates; coalescing collapses hot-key misses to one DB query; ingestion is unaffected since it's a separate, Kafka-buffered path.
> 🗣️ In plain words: "The popular stuff is cached at the edge and its counters are spread out, so a spike on a few stories doesn't melt one server."
> 💬 "Hot keys are the real risk in a spike, so I shard counters and coalesce misses — the edge eats the rest."

**❓ "Can the same story ever appear twice, or two real stories get merged into one?"**
The mechanism: dedup uses multiple signals and requires agreement, so high text-similarity alone won't merge two stories with different entities/timestamps; the atomic signature claim prevents duplicate clusters; an async re-cluster job heals borderline cases both ways.
> 🗣️ In plain words: "We need several clues to agree before merging, so we rarely double-show and rarely wrongly combine — and a background pass fixes the edge cases."
> 💬 "Multi-signal merging is exactly to avoid the two failure modes — duplicates and false merges."

**❓ "How do you stop someone from gaming `/trending` with bot views?"**
The mechanism: view events carry an idempotency key `(user, article, minute)` and the server stamps time; per-user and per-IP rate caps plus a spam filter on the event stream drop inflated traffic before it reaches the counters; counts are velocity-with-decay so a brief burst doesn't dominate.
> 🗣️ In plain words: "A bot can't just spam taps — we ignore repeats, cap per user, and score by genuine rate of rise."
> 💬 "Trending is server-scored from rate-capped, deduped events — there's no client-supplied number to forge."

**❓ "How do you make sure no one can forge a view or tamper with ranking via the API?"**
The mechanism: caller identity comes from the JWT/session, never client-supplied ids; the view endpoint accepts only `articleId` and stamps server-time and server-derived weight; ranking scores are computed server-side and never accepted from the request.
> 🗣️ In plain words: "The server decides who you are and what a view is worth — the app can't send us a score or a fake timestamp."
> 💬 "Nothing that affects ranking is ever trusted from the client."

**❓ "What's the most expensive part of this system, and how would you cut it?"**
The mechanism: CDN/egress for serving ~1B reads/day is the top line item, then the Elasticsearch cluster; the cut is pushing more read traffic to the edge (snippet payloads, optimized images) and tiering old ES indices to cheaper cold storage.
> 🗣️ In plain words: "Serving the bytes to readers costs the most, not the database — so we cache hard at the edge and keep payloads small."
> 💬 "At a billion reads a day, egress and search dominate — the database is a rounding error."

**❓ "How would you detect a source that silently stopped publishing?"**
The mechanism: each Source tracks `last_success_at` and parse-success rate; a monitor alarms when a source hasn't produced within N expected intervals or when parse-success drops, catching both an outage and a silent format change; failed items land in a dead-letter queue for inspection.
> 🗣️ In plain words: "We notice when a publisher goes quiet past its normal rhythm and page someone, instead of just losing their coverage."
> 💬 "Silent failure is the scary one, so I alarm on *absence*, not just errors."

**❓ "How would you add user personalization — a feed of followed categories/keywords?"**
The mechanism: store user follows; build a personalized feed by querying the existing category/tag indexes filtered to the user's interests, cached per-user in Redis; reuse the same ranking but skip the shared CDN cache for personalized pages. Heavy users can get a precomputed feed via fan-out on the ingestion event.
> 🗣️ In plain words: "Personalization is just the same feed, filtered to what you follow and cached per person — the plumbing's already there."
> 💬 "I built the taxonomy and event stream so personalization is a filter on top, not a rebuild."

**❓ "How would you handle breaking-news that must appear within seconds, not a minute?"**
The mechanism: a fast-lane — sources supporting push (PubSubHubbub/webhooks) bypass the polling schedule and go straight onto a priority Kafka topic with a dedicated normalizer, and breaking items can be flagged to invalidate `/latest` cache immediately rather than waiting for TTL.
> 🗣️ In plain words: "For sources that can push, we skip the polling wait and let the story jump the queue, even busting the cache so it shows instantly."
> 💬 "Breaking news gets a fast lane — push ingestion plus an immediate cache invalidation."

**❓ "How do you do content moderation / spam detection on ingested articles?"**
The mechanism: the classifier stage also runs a spam/quality model; low-quality or flagged content is held in a review state (not shown) and routed to a moderation queue; signals like source reputation and duplicate-spam patterns feed it; decisions feed back to retrain.
> 🗣️ In plain words: "The same step that tags articles also scores quality, and anything sketchy is held back for review rather than shown."
> 💬 "Moderation rides the existing classify stage — quality scoring plus a human review queue for the gray area."

**❓ "How do you monitor and retry failed ingestions?"**
The mechanism: retries use exponential backoff; poison items go to a dead-letter queue; dashboards track per-source success rate, Kafka consumer lag, and DLQ depth, with burn-rate alarms; idempotent dedup keys make every retry safe from duplicates.
> 🗣️ In plain words: "Failures retry with growing delays, the truly broken ones get quarantined for a human, and we watch source health and queue backlog with alarms."
> 💬 "Retry with backoff, dead-letter the poison, alarm on lag and silence — and retries are idempotent so they never duplicate."

> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "A news aggregator continuously pulls articles from thousands of sources, de-duplicates and tags them, and serves fast ranked feeds to millions of readers. The design splits into two halves. The read half is a layered cache — a CDN and Redis turn about thirty-five thousand reads a second into under two thousand that touch a database, with read replicas behind that and a separate stream processor maintaining the trending leaderboard from view events, plus Elasticsearch for ranked search. The write half is an ingestion pipeline where the hardest problem is near-duplicate detection: the same wire story runs across hundreds of outlets, so I fingerprint each article with SimHash, find candidates with locality-sensitive hashing, confirm with multiple signals, and make create-or-join-a-cluster atomic so concurrent copies collapse into one. The two halves connect through Kafka and change-data-capture, which keep the caches, search index, and trending counts in sync without dual writes. Every hard decision traces back to one principle: reads outnumber writes a thousand to one, so do the expensive work once on the write side and keep the read path cheap, cached, and eventually consistent."
